active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.root with improved KKT formulation and warm-start from current best solution
active_paradigms.description: Since scipy.optimize.root with KKT conditions already succeeded (+0.0046), enhance it by: (1) Use the current best solution as initial guess instead of random initialization. (2) Formulate the KKT system more carefully: stationarity conditions for the Lagrangian, primal feasibility for constraints, and complementary slackness. (3) Use the 'hybr' or 'lm' method with analytical Jacobian for faster convergence. (4) After solving KKT, verify and repair any slight constraint violations. The key insight is that KKT conditions directly target optimal solutions, avoiding the gradient approximation errors in the current smooth residual approach.
active_paradigms.cautions: KKT system may have multiple solutions; use current best solution as warm-start; handle numerical precision issues in complementary slackness; verify solution validity after solving

active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi with alternating optimization between centers and radii
active_paradigms.description: Implement a geometric approach using Voronoi diagrams: (1) Initialize circle centers with a good pattern (hexagonal or current best positions). (2) Compute Voronoi diagram with scipy.spatial.Voronoi to find nearest neighbors efficiently. (3) For each center, compute maximum radius as min(distance to boundary, distance to nearest Voronoi neighbor - that neighbor's radius). (4) Adjust centers by moving each toward the centroid of its Voronoi cell to potentially increase available space. (5) Alternate between computing max radii (via Voronoi) and adjusting centers until convergence. This exploits the geometric structure of the packing problem.
active_paradigms.cautions: Voronoi computation for bounded regions requires clipping; handle edge cases where centers are on boundaries; iterate until radii stabilize; verify no overlaps after each iteration

active_paradigms.idea: Use greedy incremental construction with largest-circle-first placement strategy
active_paradigms.description: Implement a constructive approach that places circles one at a time: (1) Start with empty unit square. (2) For each of 26 circles, find the position that allows the largest possible radius by sampling candidate positions and computing max radius given existing circles. (3) Compute max radius at each candidate as min(distance to boundary, distance to each existing circle - that circle's radius). (4) Place the circle at the best position found. (5) After all circles placed, use scipy.optimize.root to locally refine the configuration. This greedy approach explores different solution structures than simultaneous optimization.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: constructive placement quality, local space utilization
active_paradigms.cautions: Sampling density affects solution quality; early placements constrain later ones; use multiple random seeds for diversity; combine with local refinement after construction; handle edge cases near boundaries

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.shgo (Simplicial Homology Global Optimization) for guaranteed global search
tried_paradigms.description: Apply SHGO which uses simplicial homology to systematically explore the search space and find all local minima. Define bounds for all 78 variables and use a penalty-based objective. SHGO constructs a simplicial complex and uses homology groups to identify basins of attraction, guaranteeing convergence to the global minimum for Lipschitz continuous functions. Use options: maxiter=500, sampling_method='sobol', n=100 (sampling points). After SHGO finds the global basin, use SLSQP for local refinement.
tried_paradigms.cautions: SHGO can be slow for high-dimensional problems. Use the 'sobol' sampling method for better coverage. The minimizer_kwargs should specify SLSQP with constraints. Monitor memory usage with large n values.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.basinhopping with penalty-based constraint handling
tried_paradigms.description: Basin-hopping combines random perturbations with local minimization using an energy-based acceptance criterion (unlike dual_annealing's temperature-based approach). Implement by defining a penalty objective = -sum(radii) + large_penalty * sum(max(0, constraint_violations)^2). Use SLSQP as the local minimizer within basinhopping. The key advantage is that basinhopping can escape local minima by accepting worse solutions probabilistically, exploring the solution space more thoroughly than simple restarts. Set niter=200-500 iterations with appropriate stepsize for perturbations.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through better global exploration
tried_paradigms.cautions: The penalty weights must be large enough to enforce constraints but not so large as to cause numerical issues. Start with penalty=1000 and adjust. Use take_step callback to ensure perturbations stay in valid bounds.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.direct for deterministic global optimization
tried_paradigms.description: DIRECT (Dividing Rectangles) is a deterministic global optimization algorithm that systematically divides the search space into hyper-rectangles and identifies potentially optimal regions without using gradients or random sampling. Implement by flattening all 78 variables (26 centers × 2 + 26 radii) with bounds [0,1] for centers and [0.001, 0.5] for radii. Define objective = -sum(radii) + penalty(constraint_violations). DIRECT explores the space systematically and can find global optima that gradient-based methods miss. Use minf=1e-10 for precision.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through systematic global search
tried_paradigms.cautions: DIRECT requires scipy 1.11+. The algorithm can be slow for high dimensions (78 variables). Use voltol=1e-8 to control termination. The penalty formulation must be smooth enough for DIRECT's sampling strategy.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with Powell method and adaptive penalty weights
tried_paradigms.description: Powell's conjugate direction method is derivative-free and can handle non-smooth regions better than gradient-based methods. Implement an adaptive penalty approach: start with moderate penalty weights, run Powell optimization, then increase penalties and re-optimize from the previous solution. This gradual enforcement allows the optimizer to first find a good region then refine to satisfy constraints. Use method='Powell' with options={'maxiter': 10000, 'ftol': 1e-12}. The key advantage is robustness to the non-smooth constraint boundaries.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through derivative-free robust optimization
tried_paradigms.cautions: Powell doesn't handle bounds directly, so include bounds in the penalty function. Use multiple random initializations since Powell is a local optimizer. The adaptive penalty schedule should start with penalty=100 and increase by factor of 10 each round for 3-4 rounds.

tried_paradigms.description: The trust-constr method uses a trust-region approach that handles non-linear constraints more robustly than SLSQP's active-set method. It's particularly effective for problems with non-convex constraints like circle packing. Define constraints using NonlinearConstraint objects with sparse Jacobians for efficiency. Use the same smooth log-sum-exp formulation but let trust-constr handle the constraint satisfaction directly rather than through penalty terms.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Primary sum of radii through better constraint handling
tried_paradigms.cautions: Trust-constr requires explicit constraint Jacobians for best performance. Use 'lsmr' linear solver for large sparse systems. Increase maxiter to 1000 and set gtol/xtol to 1e-12 for tight convergence.

tried_paradigms.description: Basinhopping combines random perturbations with local minimization, effectively performing Monte Carlo search over local minima. It can escape local minima that trap gradient-based methods. Configure with niter=200-500, use custom step-taking that respects bounds (perturb centers within [0.05,0.95] and radii proportionally), and use SLSQP as the local minimizer. Add an accept test that rejects configurations with constraint violations.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Finding better local minima through global search
tried_paradigms.cautions: Basinhopping is stochastic - use a fixed random seed for reproducibility. The step size should be tuned: too small leads to slow exploration, too large wastes iterations. Use take_step callback to ensure bounds are respected.

tried_paradigms.description: Dual annealing is a simulated annealing variant that combines classical simulated annealing with a local search strategy. It's designed to find global minima in non-convex landscapes. Use it with bounds on all variables (centers in [0,1], radii in [0.001,0.5]), and pass SLSQP through minimizer_kwargs for local refinement. Set initial_temp=5230 and restart_temp_ratio=2e-5 for good exploration-exploitation balance.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Global optimum discovery through temperature-based search
tried_paradigms.cautions: Dual annealing minimizes, so negate the objective (maximize -sum_radii). Use visit=2.62 for proper annealing schedule. The no_local_search option should be False to enable local minimization. Set maxiter=2000 for sufficient exploration.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr method and explicit constraint Jacobians
tried_paradigms.description: Trust-constr uses a trust-region sequential quadratic programming (SQP) algorithm with interior point method, which is fundamentally different from SLSQP's active-set approach. Define constraints using NonlinearConstraint with explicit Jacobian functions for the 78 overlap constraints and 104 boundary constraints. The trust-region approach handles nonlinear constraints more robustously near constraint boundaries. Use sparse Jacobian representation since each overlap constraint involves only 4 center variables and 2 radius variables. Start from the hexagonal pattern but also try perturbed versions with different random seeds.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: primary sum of radii, with better local minima from different constraint handling
tried_paradigms.cautions: Trust-constr requires proper constraint Jacobians for efficiency. Use sparse matrices for the Jacobian since constraints are local. Set ftol=1e-12 and maxiter=2000. Try both 'equality' constrained formulation (r_i = max feasible) and direct optimization.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.dual_annealing with adaptive penalty weights
tried_paradigms.description: Dual annealing combines generalized simulated annealing with local search, using a different exploration mechanism than basinhopping. Implement an adaptive penalty function: start with low penalty weights and increase them geometrically during optimization. The objective becomes -sum(radii) + penalty_weight * sum(constraint_violations). Use the 'lw' local search option with L-BFGS-B. Set initial_temp=5230, restart_temp_ratio=2e-5, and visit=2.62 for good exploration. Run multiple independent optimizations and keep the best valid solution.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: global search capability to escape local minima that trap gradient-based methods
tried_paradigms.cautions: Dual annealing doesn't support constraints directly, so penalty must be carefully tuned. Start penalty_weight small (0.01) and increase by 1.5x every 1000 iterations. Ensure final solution is validated and radii are recomputed using max_radii_for_centers.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with COBYLA method for derivative-free optimization
tried_paradigms.description: COBYLA (Constrained Optimization BY Linear Approximation) builds linear approximations of the objective and constraints, then solves a trust-region subproblem. This derivative-free approach can find solutions that gradient methods miss, especially near constraint boundaries where gradients may be misleading. Define all 182 constraints explicitly as inequality constraints. COBYLA handles constraints directly without penalty functions. Use rhobeg=0.1 and rhoend=1e-8 for fine convergence. Run from multiple starting points including the hexagonal pattern and random perturbations.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: different local minima from derivative-free search that may escape gradient-based traps
tried_paradigms.cautions: COBYLA can be slower than gradient methods, so limit maxiter to 5000. The method works best when starting from feasible points, so use the hexagonal initialization with small radii. After optimization, recompute maximum radii for the final centers.
